title: N Points on a Circle

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-04-probability-theory.md

setup.narrative: N points are dropped uniformly at random on a circle.
rules: Each point is independent and uniform on the circle.
We want the probability that ALL N points lie within SOME semicircle (some half of the circle).
setup.question: What is the probability that all N points lie within some semicircle?

learningObjectives: Define event E_i = 'all N-1 OTHER points lie in the clockwise semicircle starting at point i'.
Argue the events E_1, ..., E_N are mutually exclusive AND exhaustive of the desired event.

stages.label: Define the event for each point
stages.prompt: Label the points 1 through N (in any order, say clockwise). Define event E_i = 'all OTHER N-1 points lie in the SEMI-CIRCLE going CLOCKWISE FROM point i'. What is P(E_i)?

options.text: (1/2)^(N-1).  Each of the other N-1 points independently falls in the chosen semicircle with probability 1/2.

stages.explanation: Conditional on point i's position, the other N-1 points are independent and uniform. Each lies in any specific semicircle with probability 1/2. All N-1 falling in the clockwise semicircle from i: (1/2)^(N-1).

stages.label: Choose your technique
stages.prompt: How do you combine the N events E_1, ..., E_N to get P(some semicircle covers all N points)?

options.text: Show E_1, ..., E_N are MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE and EXHAUSTIVE of the desired event. Then P(union) = sum of P(E_i) = N * (1/2)^(N-1).

stages.explanation: If we can show the E_i are pairwise disjoint AND their union is the event of interest, the probability is just the sum.

stages.label: Why the E_i are mutually exclusive
stages.prompt: Suppose all N points lie in a semicircle. Why can ONLY ONE of the E_i events be true?

options.text: If all N points fit in a semicircle, exactly ONE point i has the property that the (clockwise) semicircle starting AT i contains all the others -- the LAST point in the cluster, going clockwise.
Multiple E_i can be true simultaneously.
None of the E_i can be true.

stages.explanation: Among N points fitting in some semicircle, exactly ONE is the 'starting point' (the most counter-clockwise one in the cluster). For other choices of starting point, at least one of the points falls in the OTHER semicircle. So exactly one E_i fires.

stages.label: Combine
stages.prompt: P(some semicircle covers all N points) = P(union of E_1...E_N) = sum of P(E_i) = ?
stages.explanation: Sum of N equal disjoint probabilities of (1/2)^(N-1). Total: N * (1/2)^(N-1) = N / 2^(N-1).

stages.label: Verify on small N
stages.prompt: Sanity check: which of these match N * (1/2)^(N-1)?

options.text: N=1: 1 * 1 = 1 (any single point is trivially in a semicircle).
N=2: 2 * (1/2) = 1 (any two points always fit in some semicircle).

stages.explanation: All four formulas check out. Notably for N=2, the probability is 1 (two points always fit in some semicircle). For large N, the probability decays exponentially toward 0 -- as expected.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Generalize: replace 'semicircle' with an arc of fraction x of the circumference (0 < x < 1/2). What's the probability all N points fit in some such arc?
Why does the answer FAIL when x > 1/2? (Hint: the E_i are no longer mutually exclusive.)
Compare to the 1D analogue: N uniform points on [0, 1] -- what's the probability all fit in some interval of length 1/2?
